policy_statement: Implement Class 3 Clean Air Zone in Stockholm (2024–2030).
mechanism_description: Implementing a Class 3 Clean Air Zone reduces high-emitting vehicle traffic in the zone, lowering road-transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport activity/policy constraint → ↓ high-emitting vehicle use + ↑ cleaner fleet uptake → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city designates a Clean Air Zone and restricts, charges, or disincentivizes entry/operation of higher-emitting vehicles within a defined area, prompting mode shift, trip reduction, rerouting, and/or upgrading to cleaner vehicles.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced vehicle-kilometres from high-emitting vehicles and/or lower average emissions intensity of the on-road fleet reduces tailpipe fuel combustion and associated CO₂ (and other climate pollutants).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transportation-sector GHG emissions attributable to reduced fossil fuel use and lower per-km emissions within (and potentially beyond) the zone.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced road-transport fuel combustion leading to lower CO₂ (and related) greenhouse-gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: CAZ implementation primarily changes road-transport behavior and fleet composition, reducing fossil-fuel combustion and thus GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although CAZs are often motivated by air-quality/health outcomes and can yield co-benefits (NOx/PM reductions), the largest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions reduction from transport fuel use, not adaptation, resource-efficiency-as-such, or nature-based carbon sinks.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a CAZ merely displaces traffic to other areas, net GHG benefits may be reduced; however, the mechanism’s intended and typical dominant climate pathway remains emissions mitigation via reduced/cleaner vehicle activity. Electrification-led compliance still fits mitigation (lower upstream-adjusted GHG intensity).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any “clean air zone implementation → transport emissions reduction” mechanism necessarily operates by constraining high-emitting vehicles and lowering transport emissions, which is a mitigation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy is best subclassed as pricing, regulation, low-emission zone, congestion management, or fleet electrification support depends on policy-specific design details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/leakage via traffic diversion outside the zone or increased congestion at boundaries can erode net GHG reductions; some CAZs target local pollutants more than CO₂, but the stated mechanism here explicitly includes transport emissions reduction, supporting Mitigation as primary.
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (NOx/PM), public health benefits, reduced noise, potential congestion reduction.
instance_edge_case_notes: The phrase does not specify whether the Clean Air Zone is enforced via bans (regulatory) versus charges/fees (pricing mechanism), nor the exact vehicle standards covered by “Class 3”; classification assumes access restriction is the core instrument.
